name: Dickerson, Glenn Gilmer
motto: "Well, how about a Chesterfield? What's this for? What you got in that box?"
description: Glenn was undecided as to which department to enter, but having a natural love for horses he chose Animal Husbandry. That he has made a success of his course is shown from a letter he received from Mr. Barnum offering him a position as chief trainer of elephants, tigers, and mules which he has made his own. Glenn's hard work and great efforts and unusual ability have won for him the highest goal in "Aggadah," the King of the "Herd and Herdsmen." Next, "Dick" turned to baseball, and with a magnificent expenditure of energy and chewing tobacco, we find that he has again exceeded and was chosen manager of the 1924 team.

name: Pete
motto: "The best is yet to come."
description: If you have ever noticed a fellow walking around the campus like he has just come out of a bandbox, that's "Pete." Yes, he has two uniforms they say and he keeps one in the parade shop while he wears the other. But we must not leave him here because he has ambitions higher than just being a soldier. "Pete" is also an L. C. scribe. He has already begun to talk about those five thousand men that he expects to have after graduation. We are not a bit in doubt about "Pete" making good at any game he undertakes, because he is plenty resourceful. The Flippers.

name: Elliott, Milton Copeland
motto: "Sandy, old Lady, you no longer have to sleep."
description: Four years ago Hampton sent out another one of her stalwart men to pursue a course at V.P.I. This young man was none other than "Sandy." Then "Sandy," as all the boys knew him, is a jolly good fellow and for least of his activities is military. Consequently "Sandy," after four years, became an honored member of the Skipper organization. He is a heart-breaker with the ladies, and as the old saying goes, "He has one in every port." He has pursued the course in Chemistry and we believe that success lies ahead of him.

name: Horne, Andrew Fuller Jr.
clubs: Private Company "A," A.I.K.E., Southwest Virginia Club; Skipper (1922/23)
description: Born in southwest Virginia and of Scotch-Irish parentage. Realizing that his mother did not raise her boy to be a soldier, he has never had any love for military things, and consequently has taken great pride in being called a "Skipper." An excellent student and a consistent worker, he is one of the leading men in the Electrical Engineering class, and we know that some day he will extend the electrical world with some of his remarkable discoveries. As a friend who has never failed us in our hour of need, and as a gentleman, of mildest character and highest ideals, "Pat" has set us an example of what a true technician should be.

name: Henshaw, Marshall Darling
description: They call him Marshall at home, but we dubbed him "Steinmetz" almost before we knew his real name. No job was too hard or too disagreeable for him, provided that when he tackled it he might view his accomplishment. But since "Steinmetz" could not see the connection of his brain, he devoted study because it had no visible result. Problems were more to his liking, and laboratory experiments, under his hand, were transformed from careless repetition of the previous findings of others to real research into the secrets that lay hidden in the ancient machinery. Girls! Through the long years there has been only one, though as the bass of the college quartet, he must have had chances too many to mention. The regular Sunday morning "specials" were popular in Schenectady, and in later years that is where he will be found.

name: Gates, Thomas Woodson
clubs: Sergeant, Company "E," Mid-Virginia Club (1922-23); A. I. E. E.
Private, Company "E," Mid-Virginia Club (1923-24); A. I. E. E.; Skipper, Fallen Angel
description: "T. Wrasser" has the unusual distinction of being the only "Skipper" who has ever missed a breakfast this year. From the starry appearance of the sets who sit next to him at mass it would seem that he makes the best of his opportunity. As his name indicates, this Farmville man compacts everything from A. C. problems to the hearts of the few girls he allows to meet him. We certainly felt sorry for the few members of F. S. N. S. when the "Wrasser" was shipped to us last years ago. One of the greatest events in Tom's life while at V. P. I. was his promotion from the rank of Sergeant to that of an honourable "Skipper."

name: Haymaker, Harry Livingstone
description: When Harry entered V. P. I., Christiansburg came into its own. We are told that they had all but shot that would never do, and besides, you might wake him—it's only ten o'clock in the morning. Harry is one of those happy, light-hearted fellows, always smiling. As a chemist he is right there with the best of them, and he does mighty well as a rock-breaker; also, his only practice has been in search of leads, as Harry is somewhat of a geology student. Being the high monkey monk of the supreme order of Prevaricators, he has often entertained us with fabulous tales of his past, present, and future, and we suspect that with his highly imaginative powers he will be able to write stories that will rival those of Tarzan.

name: Friedman, Milton Levison
description: Milton came to us fresh from Memorial Tech in 1921, and at once secured advanced academic standing as a Sophomore Rat, and entitled to all rat privileges as such. For the past two years Friedman's Unparalleled One-Piece Orchestra was a permanent attraction at the Lyric. He could sway whole audiences to laughter and tears. Wagner is said to have done such on separate occasions, but we claim that Milton is the only man to do both simultaneously. Milton also qualified as expert salesman, hundreds of the corps falling for his gold-plated line concerning cameras and laundry bags. We do not know at present whether he expects to undertake the technical side; at any rate his technical training will be valuable in finding the centre of gravity of the three gift balls.

name: Rogers, Percy Lawton
clubs: Private Company "C," Y. M. C. A. Executive Council
description: And now we come to "Percy." He is a rather retiring sort of chap, and but few of us have really known him. He cares little for the honors and glories of college life, and thus we accept him for his quiet, unassuming manner. "Percy" has diligently struggled with and conquered all the mysteries of the Chemical world—V. P. I., anyway. Amgrah and Berlin would turn over in their very little graves could they but know how "Percy" has torn their pet theories and hypotheses to smithereens and tossed them to the four winds in order to make way for his great theories and laws that will in the near future "dominate" the immediate generations, causing the name of Rogers to go echoing and echoing down the Halls of Chemical Fame.

name: Lester, Hill
hometown: Culpeper, Orange and the surrounding territory
description: Lester is the type of man that gives his biographer much trouble, since it is impossible to do him justice in the allotted space. He received his early training at Woodberry Forest and with great difficulty broke the ties of Culpeper, Orange and the surrounding territory and came to us in the pursuit of a knowledge of Agriculture. As in Lester's academic work, no words are needed; his record speaks for itself. Lester has a winning way with the ladies which is evident from his picture. The letters from the numerous girls keep the post office from going out of business. Girls, beware if you should chance to meet this youth. Lester is ambitious, intellectual, industrious, and has many other qualities which are essential to success. Above all, he is a true friend.

He received his early training at Woodberry Forest and with great difficulty broke the ties of Culpeper, Orange and the surrounding territory and came to us in the pursuit of a knowledge of Agriculture. As in Lester's academic work, no words are needed; his record speaks for itself. Lester has a winning way with the ladies which is evident from his picture. name: Gray, Joseph Curtis
motto: "God's Gift"
description: After two years of Tech life during S. A. T. C., he decided to try his luck at William and Mary; but he could not resist the lure of his Alma Mater; so in the fall of 1922 he again decided to cast his lot in Techland. He has won a host of friends among the many acquaintances which he has made at Tech. When the call of the practical side of the electrical work proved too great an attraction for him, Tech lost a valued member of the Senior Class. It was with much regret that he decided to leave us, but we feel sure that he will meet success in his life work just the same.

name: King, William Irwin
motto: "Early in his life 'Baby' probably overheard someone say, 'Out of the mouths of babes and sucklings come forth words of wisdom.' At any rate he has been brought up to the adage ever since we have known him. Really such eyes and hair are rarely found in combination with such good sense. In speaking of his other winning qualities we can only say that they total a goodly sum and leave us confident that in all things which he undertakes 'Baby' will be successful."
clubs: Lieutenant and Adjutant Second Battalion, Staff
description: "Baby" matriculated in Civil and has gone happily through his four years working on all sides. In addition to all of his work he has found time to display a game of basketball which has placed him on the squad.

name: Love, Harry Morgan
description: Behold! The reader of the "Evening News," Harry entered V. P. I. with the class of '24, with a strong determination to be a Senior Private. After a lengthy conversation with the commandant as to how a company should be run the Major decided it necessary to make Harry Regimental Adjutant. Love is one of those all-around good fellows. His good nature, kindness and willingness to do for others are chief among his many admirable characteristics. One never looks without a friend as long as Harry is around. He is undecided whether to follow agricultural education or join the army. We are sure that he will make a success in either. With his many likes, we fear some day he will pull the wrong line on the right girl, and be taken up in a state of unpreparedness.

name: Allen, Calvin Lynn
description: It is hard to tell what to write about this product of the "Hill City." He is one of the quiet men of the class, but one who is known and admired by every man in the corps. Allen's personality has won for him a host of friends who will always remember him as an irreproachable character, and above all, a man. His mind includes too much to be practical to make him a star in his classes, but this should be considered an advantage rather than otherwise. If we do not know him as well as we might, it is because he has never gained popularity, choosing rather to have each friend who does not consider this an essential to true friendship.

name: Simmons, Ward Baxter
description: This smiling boy whom we have pictured here looks from the country land of parents. For nearly four years he was a valued member of our class, but unfortunately "Ward" heard the call of the great world, and with the cheer of the Christmas tide he quietly took his place in the ranks of the outside world. Although we hope to see him here as before June, we know that he did what was best anyway. If "Ward" puts forth in the affairs of the world as he did while at V.P.I., we are sure that his will be a prosperous life.

name: Mackey, Alfred Burton
motto: "What is that military-looking bird darting around with that bunch of corn? (Shriek!) That's Lieutenant Mackey. Oh, daddy, he's a sheep (shriek). Mackey, like the proverbial sailor, has a home in every port. His own statement is, 'I don't love any of them, but I am nice to them all.' Next to military, the fair sex claims place in this young man's heart. Nevertheless, Mackey takes the world as he finds it with the sunny smile that is typical of his whole nature, and this has won the hearts of those with whom he has come in contact. His achievements here have been earned by his push and not by his pull. Now as he matriculates in the School of Life we feel sure that his record here will prove but an introduction to his Book of Life."

name: Noble, Randolph Anderson
description: In the fall of 1928, there entered at this institution a seemingly unsophisticated lad who claimed East Randolph as his "headquarters." Since this metropolis is located in the midst of a prosperous farming section of the state, and since this lad expressed his desire to take the serious study of agriculture, the truth of his claims was not doubted. The climate of Randolph's college life was brightened when it became known that during his senior year he had the honor of being the son of a state senator, and we are proud to confide this bit of information to our outside friends. During the four years of his residence in Randolph, we have observed that "Tucker" has some strange power over the women. We wonder if this results from his winning personality or his blond hair.

name: Russell, John Cripplenen
clubs: Rappahannock Valley Club A. S. C. E.
description: John is one of those quiet boys. You can always tell John when he's coming, but you can't tell him much after you meet him. You don't have a choice. John is an ambitious and hard-working boy. For four years he has worked in "Iron," "Penny," Camp, and although he hasn't succeeded yet, he still tries whenever he has an opportunity. Although from Fredericksburg, John has made a good academic and military record while here, and we expect some day to hear that he has been promoted to rear chairman in a rich party. He graduates in Civil Engineering. His tall, erect form with his touch of red hair on top, will be remembered long by all. He will be making good homes and his long words. We expect to hear in future years that his work is as he would expect it.

name: Currus, Robert Turner
description: "Nat" reported at V.P.I. in the fall of '21 as a Sophomore-Rat. He is one of the young men who served their country and their state so creditably during the recent world war. Electrical Engineering is the avowed field of his ambition and very diligently has he prepared his course. He is a consistent hard worker, and his efforts are not in vain for he merely gets results as evidenced by his grades. In this serious minded chap we recognize the kind that accomplishes and we predict that he will make a great name in his chosen field. Our ambition that should not be ignored is that of being a loyal "Skipper."

name: Givens, Paul Brown
description: It still remains a mystery as to whether "Peck" was named after the "peck horn" or whether the "peck horn" was named after "Peck." Besides being a great athlete, "P. B." is quite an artist when it comes to getting smoke out of the air. "Peck's" ability to keep his opponent between him and the mat has won him great fame at Tech, as well as given him that great privilege of becoming a Monogram Club member. That gold band and those stripes, along with that senior cap, have earned many a fair demand to turn her attentions to Lieutenant Givens. In spite of the fact that he hails from Craig County and is very much in love, "P. B." is a good student, a hard worker, and he is sure to rise in the industrial world.

name: Wilkins, Roland Ward
description: Roland Ward Wilkins hails from the thriving metropolis known as Turbeville. "Pop," the last survivor of the S. A. T. C. days, has come back to us after a brief furlough of two years and is a living example of a man coming back and making good. Now don't let it slip your memory that he is a second Al Johnson and a mainstay in the 1924 Virginia Tech Mascot. "Pop" tells us that if he had lost the 1924 Virginia Tech Mascot, he would have been deeply disappointed.

name: Savers, Robert Craig Kent
description: Buttercup appears to be a very quiet and conservative member of our class, but those who know him best can tell you that he doesn't fail to come out strong in a while and show a good sense of humor through his dry wit. He tells us that he was born about planting time, but it must have been in the 'Eighties.' He came over here around the time of his first entering Tech in 1918 as a member of the S. A. T. C., assigned to the company of the three 'H's' - Hanks, Hoge and H.... Having served a year in the S. A. T. C., 'Buttercup' decided that he was prepared for the worst, so the next year we find him working in a graded school. The following year he came and started all over with the Class of '24.

name: Pitt, Frank Patrick
description: In just one year and a half Frank was able to conquer Old Kaiser, but it took him exactly four years to conquer the family of V.P.I. But, before he had finished with the family, he had entered another conquest which is likely to occupy the remainder of his life—Mrs. Pitts came upon the scene. "Sergeant" has spent many pleasant hours talking with Brachiopoda Articulata, Teleautomata, Terebratulacea, Streptococcus Bacteria. (No, this isn't the name of her better half.) "Sergeant" has also spent considerable hours in showing the Junior just how field problems should be worked. After Sergeant Pitts has completed his task, in which he is showing his love for his country, he will launch upon the great Chemical Wonderland.

name: Kerr, James Alexander
motto: "KERR"
description: "Pat" came with us in the fall of 1920, and after long acquaintance and intimate association with him, we have come to admire and respect him as a hard and persistent worker, a loyal and sympathetic friend, generous to a fault, and a man who stands for the best at V.P.I in everything. He is popular with his classmates, with the faculty, and in fact, with all those with whom he comes in contact. Here's to "Pat," and if our recommendations carried any weight, we would give him a dandy one.

name: Dulaney, Frank Leslie
clubs: Sergeant at Arms, Many Literary Society
description: Frank L. Dulaney, better known as "Slim," is quite an agreeable chap. He hails from Madison County where the hills are numerous and steep. "Slim" was captured in one year of the "two-year aggie," but this is now quite hidden by his polish secured in the regular "Rock" course. It is predicted by his friends that "Slim's" thoughts have frequently been elsewhere than on his studies. His real friendship could place him among the leaders of his community. "Slim" may be tall, but his accomplishments will overshadow his height.

name: Abernathy, Charles Clarence
description: If you have never become acquainted with "Abbey" you must be the loser. His sterling worth, freedom of character, with his winsome disposition and personality, win for him the admiration of all those with whom he comes in contact. This superb quality is proved by the fact that he was selected to represent his Alma Mater at conventions and various contests. He is a member of the Royal order of "Sons," meaning heresy of women; but upon noticing the frequency of mail from Dublin we wonder how he explains it. "Abbey" is pursuing the course in Agricultural Education and we have no fear for his success as already he has begun to extend his interest and knowledge into Canada.

name: Fulton, William Penn
description: Everybody knows "Bill." He comes from Patrick, the country in which the lucky "Fairy stories" are found, and we think he must have been endowed with luck by the same fairy. "Bill's" preparatory training at Randolph-Macon Academy was cut short by his entrance into the World War. After familiarizing himself with London and "Gay Paris," he returned to America and continued his cosmopolitan career in the western states. But worldly knowledge was not sufficient for him; so he decided to re-enter school. Work does not interfere with "Bill's" pleasure as was proved by his affiliation with the "Three Broomsticks." We know he will reach that famous goal known as success, in due time.

name: Lee, Roy Foster
description: Soon after the armistice, when discharges and 160 banns were all the rage, this enterprising member of our class decided to accept the educational aid offered by our widely known and well-liked Uncle Sam. Right then the army lost a good man and V. P. L. gained a splendid student. Foster has made many close friends while here, and although he has not been an outstanding figure on the campus, his classmates and those who knew him best, appreciate his worth and admire him for his readiness to always help those whom he can. Although we hate to lose this friend, we can comfort ourselves with the thought that there shall grow that which we love, and be the better for our loss.

name: Edmonson, William Harry
description: After spending two years at Bridgewater College, Harry came to live with us at Tech in 1921. By his big heartedness, his many acts, his ready smile, and pleasing personality, we have learned to know him. Nothing pleases him more than to do a kind, helpful deed for a fellow student. He has a fine sense of honor and loyalty, a clean character and the highest ideals. In short, then, he is the embodiment of a real man. He has been a diligent worker and has made a good record as a student. Here, we congratulate Mrs. Edmonson on her selection of a husband. And now, Harry, we have no vocabulary which can express our good wishes to you in your life work. We hate to part with such a friend, but we shall not forget you.

name: Starnes, William Herbert
description: He is a good man. He has become serious in his senior year. He is a good student.

name: Strouth, William Walter
description: Another one of our class who was "over there" before he came up here. Strouth did his bit with the 88th Division which fought with such valor in the Western Normandy during which offensive he was seriously wounded. We remember only black powder when we had to leave camp, how we first came to know Strouth and how we finally changed him for his promotion to secure his college education in spite of the fact that he had already embarked upon the task of matriculating. He chose Mechanical Engineering for his vocation and has kept continually forging ahead. In time we find a concentrated, hard working leader, disciplined, but not a bore, serious minded, but always ready with a smile or jolly laugh.
